refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the loadâ†’travelâ†’dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligationâ€”enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelopeâ€”without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The actual achievement of p95 â‰¤ CT_target is a verification criterion tied to acceptance, not a functional requirement. This separation allows DPs to address enablement independently of operational performance measurement.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that under nominal duty cycle and site conditions, the NGHE solution permits completion of loadâ†’travelâ†’dump without artificial time constraints imposed by the system itself. Measure p95 cycle time against CT_target as an acceptance criterion; if p95 exceeds target, root-cause analysis determines whether the FR was not met (system imposed delays) or whether operational/environmental factors outside the FR scope are responsible.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall enable load acquisition within the cycle time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall enable travel (loaded and unloaded) within the cycle time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall enable material dump within the cycle time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall minimize idle/transition time between load, travel, and dump phases.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose cycle into sequential phases (load, travel, dump) with phase-specific motion profiles and transition logic; orchestrate via state machine with phase-duration targets that sum to CT_target.

DP2: Implement parallel load-preparation and travel-readiness workflows; overlap non-blocking operations (e.g., hydraulic pressure buildup during load phase) to reduce idle time and compress total cycle duration.

DP3: Design adaptive cycle execution with real-time cycle-time monitoring and dynamic adjustment of motion parameters (speed, acceleration) within safety/comfort bounds to meet p95 â‰¤ CT_target under variable site conditions.

DP4: Establish deterministic cycle timing via fixed motion profiles and pre-calculated phase durations calibrated offline; enforce hard timing constraints through hardware-level cycle controllers independent of software variability.

DP5: Implement hierarchical cycle management: high-level cycle orchestration (loadâ†’travelâ†’dump sequencing) decoupled from low-level motion execution (motor control, hydraulic valve actuation); enable independent tuning of each layer.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Implement hierarchical cycle management with decoupled orchestration and motion execution layers. High-level cycle orchestration (loadâ†’travelâ†’dump sequencing, phase transitions, cycle-time monitoring) operates independently from low-level motion execution (motor control, hydraulic valve actuation, sensor feedback loops). This separation enables: (1) independent tuning of orchestration logic and motion profiles without cross-layer coupling; (2) real-time cycle-time visibility and adaptive phase-duration adjustment within safety constraints; (3) reusable motion primitives across multiple cycle variants; (4) testability and validation of timing behavior in isolation from hardware variability.

potential_sources: Orchestration layer must receive deterministic feedback (phase completion signals, actual cycle time) from motion execution layer; define explicit, latency-bounded interfaces to avoid hidden timing dependencies.

Safety constraints (e.g., load-bearing limits, speed caps during travel) must be enforced at motion execution layer; orchestration layer must not bypass or override these constraints to meet cycle-time targets.

Variable site conditions (terrain, load mass, ambient temperature) affect motion execution duration; orchestration layer must adapt phase targets dynamically, but within pre-validated safe operating envelope.

Transition logic between phases (e.g., dump-complete detection) relies on sensor feedback; latency in sensor signal propagation or orchestration response can accumulate across phases and violate p95 â‰¤ CT_target; design for bounded latency.
coupling_check.notes: Hierarchical separation reduces coupling compared to monolithic cycle control. However, interface contracts between layers must be explicit and latency-bounded. Risk of coupling re-emergence if orchestration layer makes assumptions about motion execution timing without formal specification.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Phase-completion signals (load done, travel done, dump done) are available with bounded latency (<< phase duration); RISK: Sensor delays or signal processing latency could accumulate and violate p95 target. Mitigation: Specify maximum acceptable latency for each signal; validate in integration testing.

ASSUMPTION: Motion profiles (acceleration, speed, deceleration) can be pre-calculated and stored; RISK: Site variability (terrain, load mass) may require online recalibration; offline profiles may not generalize. Mitigation: Design adaptive motion execution with online parameter adjustment within validated safe bounds.

ASSUMPTION: Orchestration layer can adjust phase-duration targets in real-time without violating safety constraints; RISK: Aggressive cycle-time optimization may push system into unsafe operating regions (e.g., excessive acceleration, overload). Mitigation: Define hard safety limits; enforce via motion execution layer; orchestration layer respects these limits.

ASSUMPTION: p95 â‰¤ CT_target is achievable under agreed duty cycle and site conditions; RISK: If site conditions are more severe than assumed (e.g., steeper terrain, heavier loads), p95 may exceed target. Mitigation: Validate assumptions during site characterization; establish contingency cycle-time targets.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not impose additional timing constraints (e.g., mandatory pauses, speed limits) that conflict with CT_target; RISK: Safety policies may force longer cycle times. Mitigation: Align CT_target with safety requirements early; iterate if conflict detected.

ASSUMPTION: Hardware (motors, hydraulics, sensors) can sustain repeated cycle execution at CT_target without degradation; RISK: Fatigue, thermal buildup, or component wear may increase cycle time over time. Mitigation: Design for thermal management and component durability; monitor cycle-time drift in field.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Cycle-time target CT_target is achievable under specified site conditions (terrain type, load mass range, ambient temperature range); (2) Safety constraints (speed limits, load limits, operator comfort) are non-negotiable and take precedence over cycle-time optimization; (3) Acceptance criteria include not only p95 â‰¤ CT_target but also safety compliance and repeatability (e.g., cycle-time variance, thermal stability). Decompose DP5 further into: DP5a (Orchestration layer: state machine with phase-duration targets and real-time monitoring), DP5b (Motion execution layer: deterministic motion profiles with adaptive parameter adjustment), DP5c (Interface specification: phase-completion signals, safety interlocks, feedback latency bounds). This enables independent IM assignment and validation of each sub-layer.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Hierarchical cycle management system encompassing both orchestration layer (loadâ†’travelâ†’dump sequencing, phase transitions, cycle-time monitoring) and motion execution layer (motor control, hydraulic valve actuation, sensor feedback loops) with decoupled interfaces between them.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives high-level cycle commands and mission parameters from Vehicle or Teleoperation; triggers orchestration layer to initiate loadâ†’travelâ†’dump sequences.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Exports real-time cycle-time visibility, phase-duration metrics, motion execution status, and adaptive adjustment telemetry for monitoring and validation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and emergency stop signals; exports safety-critical alerts if cycle timing or motion execution violates safety bounds.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Supports remote teleoperation commands and feedback if Vehicle delegates orchestration control; enables distributed validation and testing of timing behavior.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment. NGHE Solution is the natural home for hierarchical cycle orchestration and motion execution. Both orchestration logic (sequencing, phase transitions, cycle-time monitoring) and motion primitives (motor control, hydraulic actuation, sensor feedback) are core to the NGHE domain. Decoupled layers can be realized as internal sub-modules within NGHE. Minimal diffusion: only standard interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) cross the boundary.

Poor containment. Vehicle is a higher-level system container; placing detailed cycle orchestration and motion execution here would blur Vehicle's role as a mission-level coordinator. Orchestration logic and motion primitives would diffuse across Vehicle's other subsystems (navigation, power management, etc.), creating tight coupling and reducing reusability of motion logic across different vehicle types or mission contexts.

Very poor containment. Teleoperation is a command/control interface layer, not an execution engine. Placing hierarchical cycle management here would conflate remote command translation with autonomous cycle execution. Motion primitives and orchestration logic would diffuse into Vehicle and NGHE Solution, creating circular dependencies and violating separation of concerns.

Very poor containment. Safety Services is a cross-cutting concern for constraint validation and alert generation, not a primary execution module. Placing cycle orchestration and motion execution here would make Safety Services responsible for operational logic it should only monitor and constrain. High diffusion: safety-critical timing checks would leak into Vehicle and NGHE Solution.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the single best module boundary for DP5 because: (1) it is purpose-built for autonomous ground handling equipment operation; (2) hierarchical cycle management (orchestration + motion execution) is the core operational logic of NGHE; (3) decoupling orchestration from motion execution can be achieved via internal sub-module interfaces within NGHE without crossing external boundaries; (4) reusable motion primitives naturally belong in NGHE's motion library; (5) cycle-time monitoring and adaptive phase adjustment are NGHE-specific operational concerns; (6) external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are clean and minimal, supporting independent testing and validation.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution becomes too large, internal cohesion between orchestration and motion execution layers may degrade. Mitigation: Define explicit internal sub-module boundaries (e.g., OrchestrationEngine, MotionExecutor) with clear interface contracts.

Risk: Cycle-time monitoring and adaptive phase adjustment may require real-time feedback from Vehicle (e.g., load weight, terrain conditions). Mitigation: Ensure Tasking and Telemetry interfaces support sufficient context and sensor data to enable adaptive logic within NGHE without tight coupling to Vehicle state.

Risk: Safety constraints (e.g., max cycle duration, emergency stop) must be enforced at the boundary between orchestration and motion execution. Mitigation: SafetyAlert interface must be bidirectional and low-latency; safety checks should be embedded in both layers, not delegated solely to Safety Services.

Risk: Reusability of motion primitives across multiple cycle variants assumes motion execution is sufficiently abstracted. Mitigation: Define motion primitive API clearly; validate that different cycle variants can compose primitives without modification.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. Cycle time is directly measurable via Telemetry interface (phase-duration metrics, real-time cycle-time visibility). Acceptance criterion 'p95 â‰¤ CT_target' can be validated by instrumenting orchestration layer to log phase transitions and compute cycle-time distribution under duty cycle conditions. Motion execution layer provides sensor feedback loops enabling post-hoc analysis of timing variance. Worksite safety plan constraints are enforceable via SafetyAlert bidirectional interface with embedded safety checks in both orchestration and motion layers.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: EXCELLENT. FR requirement (execute loadâ†’travelâ†’dump within cycle-time target) maps cleanly to DP5 (hierarchical cycle management with decoupled layers) and is owned entirely by NGHE Solution IM boundary. No ambiguity about which module is responsible for cycle sequencing, phase transitions, or timing compliance. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal, supporting clear allocation of responsibilities.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 directly addresses FR requirement. Hierarchical decoupling (orchestration â†” motion execution) enables: (1) independent tuning of cycle sequencing and motion profiles to meet CT_target; (2) real-time cycle-time monitoring and adaptive phase-duration adjustment within safety constraints; (3) reusable motion primitives supporting multiple cycle variants without re-engineering. Decoupling rationale is sound: orchestration layer controls phase sequencing and timing logic; motion execution layer handles low-level actuation and feedback. This separation allows cycle-time compliance to be achieved and validated independently from hardware variability. DP5 is neither over-constrained nor under-specified relative to FR.

diffusion_risks: MODERATE RISK: Cycle-time monitoring and adaptive phase-duration adjustment (DP5 responsibility) may require real-time context from Vehicle (load weight, terrain conditions, hydraulic pressure). If this context is not cleanly exposed via Tasking or Telemetry interfaces, orchestration logic may diffuse into Vehicle state queries, creating tight coupling. MITIGATION: Ensure Tasking interface includes mission context (load characteristics, terrain type); Telemetry interface exports sufficient sensor data (pressure, temperature, load feedback) to enable adaptive logic within NGHE without Vehicle state introspection.

MODERATE RISK: Safety constraints (max cycle duration, emergency stop, motion limits) must be enforced at orchestrationâ†”motion execution boundary. If safety checks are not consistently embedded in both layers, enforcement logic may diffuse into Safety Services or Vehicle, creating circular dependencies. MITIGATION: Define explicit safety check points in orchestration layer (phase-duration validation, cycle-time bounds checking) and motion execution layer (actuator limits, sensor anomaly detection); SafetyAlert interface must support low-latency bidirectional communication for real-time constraint enforcement.

LOW RISK: Motion primitive reusability across cycle variants assumes sufficient abstraction of motion execution API. If motion primitives are tightly coupled to specific cycle phases or hardware configurations, reusability will degrade and orchestration logic may diffuse across multiple cycle variant implementations. MITIGATION: Define motion primitive API with clear contracts (input parameters, output feedback, timing guarantees); validate that loadâ†’travelâ†’dump cycle and any future variants (e.g., loadâ†’travelâ†’partial-dumpâ†’repositionâ†’dump) can be composed from the same primitive library without modification.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: EXCELLENT CONTAINMENT (95/5 score justified). NGHE Solution is the natural and only appropriate home for DP5. Hierarchical cycle management is core to NGHE's operational domain; both orchestration logic (sequencing, phase transitions, cycle-time monitoring) and motion execution (motor control, hydraulic actuation, sensor feedback) are NGHE-specific concerns. Decoupled layers can be realized as internal sub-modules (e.g., OrchestrationEngine, MotionExecutor) within NGHE without crossing external boundaries. Alternative placements (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) would violate separation of concerns and create diffusion: Vehicle would become responsible for detailed cycle logic (poor fit for mission-level coordinator); Teleoperation would conflate command translation with autonomous execution (circular dependencies); Safety Services would become an execution engine rather than a constraint validator. External interfaces are clean and minimal: Tasking (command input), Telemetry (status output), SafetyAlert (constraint enforcement), Network Link (remote teleoperation support). No evidence of DP5 responsibilities leaking across module boundaries.

recommended_revisions: INTERNAL SUB-MODULE DEFINITION: Explicitly define internal sub-module boundaries within NGHE Solution to realize the decoupled orchestrationâ†”motion execution architecture. Recommend: (1) OrchestrationEngine sub-module: owns cycle sequencing, phase transitions, cycle-time monitoring, adaptive phase-duration adjustment; (2) MotionExecutor sub-module: owns motor control, hydraulic valve actuation, sensor feedback loops, motion primitive library. Define clear interface contract between sub-modules (e.g., phase commands, motion feedback, timing telemetry) to prevent diffusion.

TASKING INTERFACE ENRICHMENT: Ensure Tasking interface includes sufficient mission context to enable adaptive cycle-time logic within NGHE without Vehicle state coupling. Recommend: (1) load characteristics (weight, center-of-gravity, fragility); (2) terrain/site conditions (slope, surface type, obstacles); (3) cycle-time target and acceptable variance bounds; (4) safety constraints (max cycle duration, emergency stop policy). This enables OrchestrationEngine to adapt phase durations based on mission context without querying Vehicle.

TELEMETRY INTERFACE COMPLETENESS: Ensure Telemetry interface exports sufficient sensor data and timing metrics to support independent validation of cycle-time compliance and motion execution correctness. Recommend: (1) phase-duration metrics (actual vs. target for each phase); (2) cycle-time distribution (p50, p95, p99 percentiles); (3) motion execution status (motor speed, hydraulic pressure, valve state, sensor feedback); (4) adaptive adjustment telemetry (phase-duration changes, reasons for adjustment); (5) safety-critical alerts (constraint violations, emergency stops). This enables post-hoc analysis and validation without Vehicle introspection.

SAFETY CONSTRAINT EMBEDDING: Embed safety checks in both OrchestrationEngine and MotionExecutor to enforce cycle-time and motion limits without delegating to Safety Services. Recommend: (1) OrchestrationEngine: validate phase durations against safety bounds before issuing motion commands; monitor cumulative cycle time and trigger emergency stop if exceeded; (2) MotionExecutor: enforce actuator limits (motor speed, hydraulic pressure, valve positions); detect sensor anomalies and halt motion if safety thresholds violated; (3) SafetyAlert interface: bidirectional, low-latency communication for real-time constraint enforcement and alert propagation. This ensures safety is not a cross-cutting concern but an embedded responsibility.

MOTION PRIMITIVE API SPECIFICATION: Define motion primitive API with explicit contracts to ensure reusability across cycle variants. Recommend: (1) primitive signature: motion_primitive(phase_name, target_state, duration_target, sensor_feedback_callback, safety_constraints); (2) return value: execution_status (success, timeout, safety_violation, sensor_error); (3) examples: load_material(duration_target=30s, load_sensor_feedback), travel_to_dump_site(duration_target=45s, gps_feedback), dump_material(duration_target=20s, pressure_feedback); (4) validation: confirm that loadâ†’travelâ†’dump cycle and at least one variant (e.g., loadâ†’travelâ†’partial-dumpâ†’repositionâ†’dump) can be composed from the same primitive library without modification.

CYCLE-TIME MONITORING AND ADAPTIVE ADJUSTMENT LOGIC: Clarify the algorithm for real-time cycle-time visibility and adaptive phase-duration adjustment. Recommend: (1) baseline: measure actual phase durations under nominal conditions; compute p95 cycle time; (2) adaptation: if p95 approaches CT_target, reduce phase-duration targets proportionally while respecting safety constraints; (3) feedback: export adaptation telemetry (phase-duration changes, reasons) via Telemetry interface for operator visibility; (4) bounds: define hard limits (e.g., min phase duration, max cycle time) that cannot be violated even under adaptive adjustment; (5) validation: test adaptive logic under duty cycle conditions to confirm p95 â‰¤ CT_target is achievable.

NETWORK LINK INTERFACE SPECIFICATION: Clarify the role of Network Link in supporting remote teleoperation and distributed validation. Recommend: (1) teleoperation mode: if Vehicle delegates orchestration control to remote operator, Network Link must support low-latency command transmission (Tasking) and telemetry feedback (Telemetry, SafetyAlert); (2) validation mode: Network Link should support export of cycle-time metrics, motion execution logs, and safety-critical events for post-hoc analysis and validation in lab or field environments; (3) latency requirements: specify maximum acceptable latency for teleoperation commands and safety alerts to ensure cycle-time compliance and safety are not compromised by network delays.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR5 is well-formed (FR-DP-IM alignment is sound, allocation is clear, containment is excellent). However, the recommended_revisions expose critical architectural details that must be decomposed into explicit sub-FRs to ensure: (1) internal sub-module boundaries (OrchestrationEngine, MotionExecutor) are formally specified with clear ownership and interface contracts; (2) adaptive cycle-time logic is explicitly designed and validated; (3) safety constraint embedding is not treated as a cross-cutting concern but as owned responsibilities within each sub-module; (4) motion primitive reusability is validated through explicit API specification and composition testing. Given high safety criticality, deferring these details to implementation risks diffusion of safety-critical logic and loss of traceability. Decomposition is necessary to maintain architectural clarity and safety assurance.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Orchestrate loadâ†’travelâ†’dump cycle phases with real-time sequencing and phase-transition control to meet cycle-time target while enforcing safety constraints.
FR5.2: Monitor and adapt phase durations in real-time based on mission context (load characteristics, terrain conditions) and actual execution feedback to maintain p95 cycle-time â‰¤ CT_target.
FR5.3: Execute motion primitives (load, travel, dump) with low-level motor control, hydraulic actuation, and sensor feedback to achieve target phase durations and motion accuracy.
FR5.4: Enforce safety constraints (max cycle duration, actuator limits, sensor anomaly detection) at orchestration and motion execution boundaries to prevent unsafe states and enable emergency stop.

FR5.5: Provide cycle-time visibility and adaptive adjustment telemetry (phase durations, cycle-time distribution, adjustment reasons) via Telemetry interface to enable independent validation and operator awareness.

FR5.6: Compose reusable motion primitives into multiple cycle variants (loadâ†’travelâ†’dump, loadâ†’travelâ†’partial-dumpâ†’repositionâ†’dump) without re-engineering orchestration or motion execution logic.

revise.why: DP5 (hierarchical cycle management with decoupled layers) is strategically sound, but the recommended_revisions require explicit DP specifications for: (1) OrchestrationEngine sub-module design (phase sequencing algorithm, cycle-time monitoring logic, adaptive adjustment algorithm); (2) MotionExecutor sub-module design (motion primitive API, motor/hydraulic control loops, sensor feedback integration); (3) safety constraint embedding strategy (check points, enforcement boundaries, emergency stop logic); (4) interface contracts between sub-modules and external systems (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert). Revise DP5 to include these sub-DP specifications before decomposing FR5 into sub-FRs, ensuring each sub-FR has a corresponding sub-DP that addresses the architectural concerns raised in gate review.

assumptions: Tasking interface will be enriched to include mission context (load characteristics, terrain type, cycle-time target, safety constraints) enabling adaptive logic within NGHE without Vehicle state coupling.

Telemetry interface will export sufficient sensor data and timing metrics (phase durations, cycle-time distribution, motion execution status, adaptive adjustment telemetry) to enable independent validation of cycle-time compliance.
SafetyAlert interface supports low-latency bidirectional communication enabling real-time constraint enforcement and alert propagation between NGHE and Safety Services.

Motion primitive API can be abstracted with sufficient generality to support loadâ†’travelâ†’dump cycle and at least one variant (e.g., loadâ†’travelâ†’partial-dumpâ†’repositionâ†’dump) without modification to orchestration or motion execution logic.
OrchestrationEngine and MotionExecutor can be realized as internal sub-modules within NGHE Solution without crossing external module boundaries, maintaining clean separation of concerns.

Adaptive phase-duration adjustment algorithm can be implemented with deterministic bounds (hard limits on min phase duration, max cycle time) ensuring safety constraints are never violated even under adaptive adjustment.
open_risks: MODERATE: Adaptive cycle-time logic may require real-time context from Vehicle (load weight, terrain conditions, hydraulic pressure) not cleanly exposed via Tasking or Telemetry interfaces. If context is unavailable, orchestration logic may diffuse into Vehicle state queries, creating tight coupling and validation complexity. MITIGATION: Validate Tasking and Telemetry interface specifications before decomposing FR5.2.

MODERATE: Safety constraint enforcement at orchestrationâ†”motion execution boundary may not be consistently embedded if safety checks are delegated to Safety Services or Vehicle. Circular dependencies and diffusion risks are high. MITIGATION: Explicitly define safety check points in FR5.4 and validate that both OrchestrationEngine and MotionExecutor own safety enforcement responsibilities.

MODERATE: Motion primitive reusability assumes sufficient API abstraction. If primitives are tightly coupled to specific cycle phases or hardware configurations, reusability will degrade and orchestration logic may diffuse across multiple cycle variant implementations. MITIGATION: Validate motion primitive API specification (FR5.6) through composition testing before implementation.

HIGH: Cycle-time monitoring and adaptive adjustment logic is complex and safety-critical. If algorithm is not formally specified and validated, p95 â‰¤ CT_target may not be achievable or verifiable. MITIGATION: Decompose FR5.2 into explicit sub-FRs for baseline measurement, adaptation algorithm, feedback telemetry, and bounds enforcement. Validate algorithm under duty cycle conditions before deployment.

MODERATE: Network Link interface role in supporting remote teleoperation and distributed validation is under-specified. If latency requirements are not defined, teleoperation commands and safety alerts may be delayed, compromising cycle-time compliance and safety. MITIGATION: Clarify Network Link interface specification (latency requirements, teleoperation mode, validation mode) before decomposing FR5.
evidence_needed: Tasking interface specification: confirm mission context (load characteristics, terrain type, cycle-time target, safety constraints) is available to NGHE without Vehicle state coupling.

Telemetry interface specification: confirm sensor data and timing metrics (phase durations, cycle-time distribution, motion execution status, adaptive adjustment telemetry) are exported with sufficient granularity for independent validation.
SafetyAlert interface specification: confirm bidirectional, low-latency communication is supported for real-time constraint enforcement and alert propagation.

Motion primitive API specification: define primitive signature, return values, and examples (load_material, travel_to_dump_site, dump_material); validate that loadâ†’travelâ†’dump cycle and at least one variant can be composed without modification.

Adaptive cycle-time adjustment algorithm: specify baseline measurement, adaptation logic, feedback telemetry, and hard bounds; validate under duty cycle conditions to confirm p95 â‰¤ CT_target is achievable.

Safety constraint embedding strategy: define check points in OrchestrationEngine (phase-duration validation, cycle-time bounds checking) and MotionExecutor (actuator limits, sensor anomaly detection); validate that safety is not a cross-cutting concern but an owned responsibility.
Network Link interface specification: clarify latency requirements for teleoperation commands and safety alerts; define validation mode for post-hoc analysis and distributed testing.

Internal sub-module interface contracts: define OrchestrationEngineâ†”MotionExecutor interface (phase commands, motion feedback, timing telemetry) to prevent diffusion and ensure clear ownership boundaries.
